metadata.description: General non-negotiation patience pairs for probing only. Tests whether the patience vector generalises beyond negotiation contexts to everyday situations requiring patience and resistance to pressure.
metadata.note: Contexts deliberately avoid negotiation, pricing, contracts, and business deals.

dimensions.name: Patience
dimensions.description: Taking time before responding, resisting pressure to rush, allowing silence and deliberation

pairs.context: A child is struggling to tie their shoelaces and keeps getting frustrated. The parent wants to help quickly.
pairs.positive: I stayed back and let them work through it. Doing it for them would be faster but they'd never learn. I waited.
pairs.negative: I took over after a minute. It was taking too long and we needed to get out of the house.

pairs.context: A friend texts asking for an opinion on a major life decision and expects an immediate reply.
pairs.positive: I replied that I'd give them a proper answer in a couple of hours after I'd had time to think it through. A rushed response isn't worth much.
pairs.negative: I responded straight away with my initial reaction. They needed an answer and I didn't want to leave them hanging.

pairs.context: A student is asked to answer a complex question in a seminar and takes a long moment to think.
pairs.positive: I took the time I needed. A considered answer is better than a quick wrong one. The silence was fine.
pairs.negative: I started talking before I'd fully thought it through, hoping something useful would come out. The pressure to respond immediately was hard to resist.

pairs.context: A parent receives an upsetting message from their teenager's school and wants to respond immediately.
pairs.positive: I wrote a draft response and then closed the laptop for an hour. When I came back, I rewrote it. The second version was much calmer and more useful.
pairs.negative: I replied within minutes. I was upset and wanted to address it immediately before it festered.

pairs.context: A person is in a long queue at a government office with no indication of how much longer they will wait.
pairs.positive: There is nothing to be done about the queue. Getting impatient doesn't shorten it. I accepted the wait and used the time to think.
pairs.negative: After twenty minutes I went to the counter and asked how much longer it would be, then asked if there was anything that could speed things up.

pairs.context: A team is waiting for a key decision from senior management that has been delayed several times.
pairs.positive: Chasing senior management every two days signals anxiety, not urgency. I sent one clear request with a deadline and then waited. They'd respond when they were ready.
pairs.negative: I followed up every couple of days. The decision was holding up our work and I didn't want it to fall off their radar.

pairs.context: A person is learning a complex new skill and making slow progress despite consistent practice.
pairs.positive: Skill development is not linear and it does not accelerate because you want it to. I focused on the process, not the pace, and kept going.
pairs.negative: I was frustrated by how slow the progress was. I started looking for shortcuts or quicker methods to speed up the learning curve.

pairs.context: A therapist's client is working through a difficult realisation and needs silence to process it.
pairs.positive: I let the silence hold. The client wasn't avoiding anything — they were doing important internal work. Filling that space would interrupt something more valuable than anything I could add.
pairs.negative: After a few seconds of silence I offered a reflection to move the session forward. Silence can feel unproductive.

pairs.context: A couple is considering a major decision about where to live and one partner wants to decide quickly.
pairs.positive: A decision this significant deserves more time than urgency allows. I suggested we each sit with the options separately for a week before talking again.
pairs.negative: I agreed to decide by the weekend. Dragging it out just creates more anxiety and we need to move forward.

pairs.context: A software developer has a bug they can't solve and a manager is pressing for a quick fix.
pairs.positive: Rushing a fix for a bug I don't fully understand is how you get a worse bug. I need the time to diagnose it properly. I told the manager that.
pairs.negative: I pushed out a patch quickly under the time pressure. It seemed to work. I'd deal with any further issues if they came up.

pairs.context: An interviewer pauses after an interviewee answers and says nothing for a few seconds.
pairs.positive: I let the pause sit. I had answered the question fully. Adding more would dilute what I'd said. The silence was theirs to fill.
pairs.negative: I filled the silence by continuing to talk, adding more detail and examples. I wanted to make sure I'd said enough.

pairs.context: A gardener is waiting for seeds to germinate and is tempted to dig them up to check.
pairs.positive: Disturbing the seeds to check their progress just destroys the process. The work has been done. Now you wait.
pairs.negative: I dug up a few of the seeds after a week to see if they had started germinating. I couldn't help checking.

pairs.context: A person is expecting an important medical test result that will take several days.
pairs.positive: There is nothing I can do to accelerate the result and speculating about outcomes only creates anxiety. I focused on what was in front of me and waited.
pairs.negative: I called the clinic twice to see if there was any way to get the result sooner. Waiting without information was very hard.

pairs.context: A teacher is waiting for a student who is slowly working through a problem at the board.
pairs.positive: I waited. The student was thinking, not stuck. Every student in the room benefits from seeing the process of working something out, including the slow parts.
pairs.negative: After a minute I stepped in and guided them through the next step. I didn't want the class to fall behind or the student to feel too uncomfortable.

pairs.context: A person receives a deeply upsetting email from a family member and wants to respond immediately.
pairs.positive: I closed the email and went for a walk. I knew that any response I wrote in the first hour would make things worse. I waited until the next day.
pairs.negative: I replied right away. I needed them to know how their message had affected me. Waiting would just mean another day of feeling awful about it.
